title: Psalm 2
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander

outline.point: I. The Nations Rage in Vain (Psalm 2:1-3)
subpoints: A. 'Why' — an expression of astonishment at the futility of the nations' rebellion
B. Kings and rulers take counsel together against the Lord and His anointed
C. Acts 4:25-28 fulfills this in Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and Israel conspiring against Christ

outline.point: II. God Laughs at Their Futility (Psalm 2:4-6)
subpoints: A. He who sits in the heavens laughs — Adonai, the sovereign Lord of heaven and earth
B. He speaks to them in wrath and terrifies them in burning fury
C. 'I have set my king on Zion' — emphatic 'I'; God's sovereign decree cannot be overturned

outline.point: III. The Decree of the Lord (Psalm 2:7)
subpoints: A. 'You are my son; today I have begotten you'
B. Acts 13 and Romans 1:4 point to the resurrection as the decisive declaration of Christ's sonship
C. The Son's eternal relationship with the Father contrasted with the historical declaration at resurrection
A. 'Ask of me and I will give you the nations for your inheritance'
B. The rod of iron — Revelation 2, 12, and 19 apply this to Christ's universal rule
C. Those who submit are shepherded; those who rebel are dashed like pottery
A. 'Be wise, O kings; be instructed, you judges of the earth'
B. Serve the Lord with fear and rejoice with trembling
C. Blessed are all those who put their trust in Him — the blessed of Psalm 1 finds its answer here

summary:
In this Sunday School lesson on Psalm 2, Elder Scott Hollander presents the Psalm as the first and most quoted Messianic Psalm in the New Testament, exploring its literary structures — parallelism, chiasm, and inclusion — to help students engage more deeply with Hebrew poetry in their own reading. The lesson works through the Psalm's four movements: the futile rage of the nations against the Lord and His anointed; God's sovereign laughter and the proclamation of His king on Zion; the divine decree declaring the Son's identity and inheritance; and the summons to the kings of the earth to submit or be destroyed. Elder Hollander grounds the Psalm in the Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7, showing that while it had a historical dimension at every Davidic coronation, its prophetic horizon always pointed to Jesus Christ.

The New Testament use of Psalm 2 is developed throughout, with Acts 4:25-28 confirming that the conspiring kings and rulers were fulfilled in Herod, Pilate, and both Gentiles and Israel plotting against Jesus — yet doing only what God's hand and purpose had determined. Acts 13 and Romans 1:4 identify the resurrection as the public declaration of Christ's sonship, the event that proclaimed His universal kingship. The lesson closes with Psalm 2:10-12, which calls the nations to submit to the Son's easy yoke or be dashed to pieces, and notes the Psalm's literary inclusion with Psalm 1: the 'blessed' of Psalm 1:1 is answered by the 'blessed' of Psalm 2:12 — those who take refuge in the Son.
transcript:
## Introduction: A Royal, Coronation, and Messianic Psalm

Psalm 2 is a royal Psalm, a coronation Psalm, and the first Messianic Psalm of the Psalter. It prophesies the triumph of Messiah and the Messianic Kingdom, and it is the most quoted and alluded-to Psalm in the entire New Testament.

This morning I want us to consider its literary structures. One of the main components of Hebrew poetry is **parallelism** — the same thought stated twice in related or escalating ways. What looks redundant actually increases specificity or emphasis. There is also **chiasm** and **inclusion** — literary framing devices.

Psalm 1 begins: *“Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the ungodly.”* Psalm 2 closes: *“Blessed are all those who put their trust in Him.”* These two Psalms are bound together by this inclusion as a unit. Psalm 1 contrasts the way of the righteous with the way of the wicked; Psalm 2 is a full-length portrait of the ultimate Righteous One — the Lord Jesus Christ.

## The Davidic Covenant: Foundation for the Psalm

Because Psalm 2 is a royal and Messianic Psalm, it has a significant relationship to the Davidic Covenant. Yahweh speaks to Nathan concerning David:

> *“I will set up your seed after you, who will come from your body, and I will establish his kingdom. He shall build a house for My name, and I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever. I will be his Father, and he shall be My son… Your throne shall be established forever.”* (2 Samuel 7:12–16)

You will hear elements of this covenant echoing throughout Psalm 2 and in the New Testament’s use of the Psalm.

## The Nations Rage in Vain (Psalm 2:1–3)

> *“Why do the nations rage, and the people plot a vain thing? The kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers take counsel together, against the LORD and against His Anointed.”* (Psalm 2:1–2)

The first word is *why* — literally, “to what end?” This is not fear or surprise but astonishment at their futility. The word *rage* describes the snorting and stamping of an agitated horse — a restless tumult. The word translated *people* may carry a connotation of a *warring people*, numbering their troops for battle.

Notably, the same Hebrew word translated *meditate* in Psalm 1:2 — *“in His law he meditates day and night”* — appears here as *plot*. In Psalm 1 it describes righteous reflection on God’s Word; here it describes the kings’ hostile military planning against the Lord and His Anointed.

The kings of the ancient Near East considered themselves appointed by their deities. But Yahweh is not merely one territorial god — He is God of heaven and earth. The New Testament identifies the fulfillment:

> *“For truly against Your holy Servant Jesus, whom You anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the people of Israel, were gathered together to do whatever Your hand and Your purpose determined before to be done.”* (Acts 4:27–28)

They were plotting in vain — not only because they could not overthrow the Lord and His Anointed, but because Christ’s crucifixion was God’s redemption plan all along.

The nations want to cast off His bonds and cords. But what they are rejecting is not a tyrant’s yoke — it is the yoke of the one who says:

> *“Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest… My yoke is easy and My burden is light.”* (Matthew 11:28–30)

## God Laughs and Sets His King on Zion (Psalm 2:4–6)

> *“He who sits in the heavens shall laugh; the Lord shall hold them in derision. Then He shall speak to them in His wrath, and distress them in His deep displeasure: ‘Yet I have set My King on My holy hill of Zion.’”* (Psalm 2:4–6)

The word *Lord* here is *Adonai* — the divine sovereign Master. This is anthropopathic language: God laughing at the futile efforts of man. He is not distressed by their plotting. When He does speak, it is in burning wrath that terrifies them.

Then He makes His sovereign declaration: *“Yet I — I have set My King on My holy hill of Zion.”* The emphatic *I* shuts down all dispute. God has made His decision and it stands.

## The Son’s Sonship Declared (Psalm 2:7)

> *“I will declare the decree: The LORD has said to Me, ‘You are My Son; today I have begotten You.’”* (Psalm 2:7)

The New Testament points to the resurrection as the decisive public declaration of Christ’s sonship:

> *“Concerning His Son Jesus Christ our Lord, who was born of the seed of David according to the flesh, and declared to be the Son of God with power according to the Spirit of holiness, by the resurrection from the dead.”* (Romans 1:3–4)

There was never a time when the second person of the Trinity was not the Son of God. He has always existed as such. But the resurrection proclaimed His sonship emphatically before the world. Acts 13 draws out the same messianic fulfillment from this verse.

## The Son’s Inheritance (Psalm 2:8–9)

> *“Ask of Me, and I will give You the nations for Your inheritance, and the ends of the earth for Your possession. You shall break them with a rod of iron; You shall dash them to pieces like a potter’s vessel.”* (Psalm 2:8–9)

This asking for the inheritance parallels Joshua 1, where Yahweh told Joshua: I have already given you this land — now go and take possession of it. Revelation 2, 12, and 19 all apply the rod of iron to Christ’s universal rule. Those who submit are shepherded; those who rebel are dashed like pottery.

## The Call to Submit (Psalm 2:10–12)

> *“Now therefore, be wise, O kings; be instructed, you judges of the earth. Serve the LORD with fear, and rejoice with trembling. Kiss the Son, lest He be angry, and you perish in the way, when His wrath is kindled but a little. Blessed are all those who put their trust in Him.”* (Psalm 2:10–12)

The Psalm closes with a warning and an invitation. Kings and rulers may submit and be blessed, or they may be crushed. The word translated *Son* in verse 12 is the Aramaic word *bar* rather than the Hebrew *ben* — a subtle reminder that this Psalm speaks not only to Israel but to all nations.

And the Psalm closes with the same word it began with alongside Psalm 1: *blessed*. Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the ungodly (Psalm 1:1). Blessed are all those who put their trust in Him (Psalm 2:12). The inclusion is complete.

## Conclusion

I hope this lesson helps you appreciate the richness of Hebrew poetry — its parallelism, its messianic resonance, its literary structures. As you read the Psalms on your own, look for these things. Look for the messianic implications alongside the historical setting. And look for where the New Testament draws on and interprets the Psalms for us.
